name: Sierra de Guara – circular via Naval, Lecina and Puerto de Mondo
name_original: Sierra de Guara – circular por Naval, Lecina y Puerto de Mondo
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Abizanda traverse the diverse pre-Pyrenean landscapes of the Aragon Pyrenees, characterized by the Sierra de Guara and Sierra de Arbe mountain ranges. The terrain features significant elevation gains, challenging climbs, and dramatic canyons, offering a demanding cycling experience. Roads often have low traffic, providing an immersive ride through a landscape sculpted by water and rock formations. This region presents a network of routes with varied topography, including mountain cols and slopes with gradients exceeding 12%. Best road cycling routes around Abizanda The most popular road cycling route is Sierra de Guara – circular via Naval, Lecina and Puerto de Mondo, a 40.7 miles (65.5 km) trail that takes 3 hours 50 minutes to complete. This difficult route features substantial elevation gain through the Sierra de Guara. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Mesón de Fuebla – Campodarbe Spring loop from Almazorre, a difficult 39.1 miles (62.9 km) path. This route navigates challenging pre-Pyrenean terrain with over 1100 meters of elevation gain. Local road cyclists also love the Hermitage of Santa Brígida – Church of Banastón loop from Mediano, a 33.2 miles (53.4 km) trail leading through varied landscapes and historical points, often completed in about 2 hours 48 minutes. Road cycling around Abizanda is defined by pre-Pyrenean landscapes, dramatic canyons, and challenging mountain cols. The network offers options primarily for difficult ability levels, with significant elevation gains. The routes in Abizanda are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.9 stars from over 6 reviews. More than 550 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Abizanda's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What is the general difficulty level of road cycling routes around Abizanda?
A: Road cycling routes in the Abizanda region are primarily designed for difficult ability levels, featuring significant elevation gains and challenging climbs. The terrain includes numerous mountain cols and slopes with gradients exceeding 12%, making for a demanding yet rewarding experience.

Q: How many road cycling routes are available in the Abizanda area?
A: There are 9 road cycling routes documented around Abizanda on komoot, with 7 of these classified as difficult and 2 as moderate. These routes offer a variety of distances and elevation profiles across the pre-Pyrenean landscapes.

Q: What kind of landscapes can I expect to see while road cycling near Abizanda?
A: You will experience diverse and dramatic pre-Pyrenean landscapes, dominated by the Sierra de Guara and Sierra de Arbe mountain ranges. Expect stunning views among rock giants, deep canyons, and dramatic rock massifs, especially within the Sierra y Cañones de Guara Natural Park. Some routes also offer 'spaghetti western landscapes' and panoramic vistas from points like the Mirador del Vero.

Q: Are there any circular road cycling routes around Abizanda?
A: Yes, several routes are circular. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e926640615/sierra-de-guara-circular-via-naval-lecina-and-puerto-de-mondo" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sierra de Guara – circular via Naval, Lecina and Puerto de Mondo&lt;/a&gt; is a demanding 65.5 km loop with substantial elevation gain. Another option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38168652" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Mesón de Fuebla – Campodarbe Spring loop from Almazorre&lt;/a&gt;, covering 62.9 km through challenging pre-Pyrenean terrain.

Q: What historical or cultural landmarks can I explore along the road cycling routes?
A: The region is rich in cultural heritage. Routes often pass through charming, picturesque towns and medieval villages like Ainsa and Alquézar, which are recognized among Spain's most beautiful. You can also see historical structures such as Abizanda Castle, and ancient paths that once connected these settlements. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/1675247" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Hermitage of Santa Brígida – Church of Banastón loop from Mediano&lt;/a&gt;, for example, leads through varied landscapes and historical points.

Q: What natural attractions are near the road cycling routes in Abizanda?
A: The area is home to several natural wonders. You can find the unique geology of the Sierra y Cañones de Guara Natural Park, with highlights such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4446051" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Entremón Gorge&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/961906" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Vero Gorge&lt;/a&gt;. Other notable natural monuments include &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3363730" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Fuentes de Lecina&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2984940" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;The Portal de Cunarga: Stone Arch&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What do other cyclists say about road cycling in Abizanda?
A: The road cycling routes around Abizanda are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.9 stars from over 6 reviews. Cyclists often praise the stunning pre-Pyrenean landscapes, the challenging terrain, and the low traffic on many roads, which enhances the immersive cycling experience.

Q: Are there any less crowded road cycling routes in the Abizanda area?
A: Many roads in the Abizanda region are known for having very little traffic, which significantly enhances the cycling experience. This allows for a more peaceful ride through the dramatic landscapes. Routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/6954564" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puerto de Mondot – Naval loop from Abizanda&lt;/a&gt; offer extensive stretches of quiet roads.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Abizanda?
A: The region's diverse climate makes spring and autumn ideal for road cycling, offering pleasant temperatures for tackling the significant elevation gains. Summers can be hot, especially in the canyons, while winters may bring colder conditions at higher altitudes.

Q: Are there any family-friendly road cycling options around Abizanda?
A: While many routes around Abizanda are classified as difficult due to significant elevation gains and challenging terrain, some shorter, less strenuous sections or local roads within villages might be suitable for families. However, the primary network of routes is geared towards experienced road cyclists.

Q: Where can I find parking when planning a road cycling trip to Abizanda?
A: Parking is generally available in the main towns and villages that serve as starting points for routes, such as Abizanda itself or Almazorre. It's advisable to check specific village amenities for designated parking areas, especially for longer rides.

Q: Are there any cafes or pubs along the road cycling routes in Abizanda?
A: Routes often pass through small, picturesque towns and medieval villages. These villages, such as Ainsa and Alquézar, typically offer local amenities including cafes and pubs where cyclists can refuel and rest. It's a good idea to plan your route to include these stops.
tips: Naval (Nabal in Aragonese) has a population of 270 inhabitants (INE 2017); Due to its geographical location at the foot of the Sierra de Arbe, it was until 1989, the year of the construction of a new route on the A-138 road that avoided passing through the Alto del Pino pass and therefore through the Villa de Naval, the natural way of communication of the Somontano with the Sobrarbe and with France.

This majestic holm oak, 16.5 m tall and 28 m in diameter, was named European Tree of the Year 2021 after having also been awarded at regional and state level. It is affectionately called "La Castañera" because of the number of acorns it produced in the harshest winters, up to 600 kilos.

The oak has its own legend that you can read here: https://www.huescalamagia.es/blog/la-carrasca-de-lecina/
